sections.heading: Public and private sources were still nearly even overall
sections.body: Among modern contraceptive users aged 15 to 49 years, 51.0 percent got their method from the public sector in 2025. Another 41.8 percent used the private medical sector, and 7.2 percent used another source. The public-sector share was almost the same as in 2022, when it was 50.1 percent. The private medical sector fell from 46.3 to 41.8 percent, while other sources outside medical channels doubled from 3.6 to 7.2 percent.

sections.heading: IUDs, injectables, and implants mostly came from public providers
sections.body: In 2025, the methods most tied to the public sector were implants at 93.9 percent, IUDs at 90.4 percent, and injectables at 89.0 percent. These shares were already high in 2022, but they still increased: implants from 90.0 percent, IUDs from 88.3 percent, and injectables from 85.3 percent. This points to a steady pattern where methods that usually need clinical insertion or administration continued to depend heavily on public providers.

sections.heading: Different methods used different public facilities
sections.body: The public sector was not one single source. In 2025, female sterilization was mostly done through government hospitals at 67.9 percent, while injectables were mostly obtained through barangay health stations at 57.0 percent. Implants were mainly split between rural or urban health centers and lying-in clinics at 35.6 percent, and barangay health stations at 34.7 percent. IUDs were spread across several public sources: 47.3 percent from government hospitals, 23.8 percent from health centers or lying-in clinics, and 18.6 percent from barangay health stations.

sections.heading: Pill users still mostly went to pharmacies
sections.body: Pills stood out from the methods that relied more on the public sector. In 2025, 67.0 percent of pill users got the method from the private medical sector, almost all through pharmacies at 66.0 percent. The public sector accounted for 22.8 percent, mostly through barangay health stations at 15.1 percent. From 2022 to 2025, pharmacy sourcing fell from 69.6 to 66.0 percent, while shop or store sourcing rose from 5.2 to 10.1 percent.

sections.heading: Male condom sourcing moved away from pharmacies
sections.body: Male condoms were still more tied to private and retail sources than most other methods, but the mix of sources changed a lot. Pharmacy sourcing dropped from 71.9 percent in 2022 to 54.2 percent in 2025, while shop or store sourcing rose from 12.6 to 29.2 percent. Other source channels also became more common, increasing from 13.8 to 33.1 percent. This included sourcing from a friend or relative, which rose from 0.4 to 3.9 percent. Because the source-row estimates for male sterilization and other methods are suppressed, condoms are the main male-oriented method that can be interpreted from this table.

sections.heading: Female sterilization stayed centered in hospitals
sections.body: Female sterilization had one of the steadiest source patterns. Public-sector sourcing was 73.5 percent in 2025, almost the same as 73.0 percent in 2022. Government hospitals accounted for 67.9 percent of sourcing in 2025, while private hospitals, clinics, or lying-in clinics accounted for another 25.4 percent. This sets female sterilization apart from pills and condoms, which were mostly retail-based, and from injectables and implants, where barangay and health-center channels were much more prominent.

takeaways: In 2025, modern contraceptive users were almost evenly split between public-sector sources and the private medical sector.
IUDs, injectables, and implants relied heavily on public providers, while pills and male condoms were much more tied to pharmacies, shops, and other nonpublic sources.
From 2022 to 2025, the clearest shift was toward sources outside medical channels, especially for male condoms and, to a lesser extent, pills.
